nodes.label: Time & Motion Study
nodes.description: Systematic observation and quantification of every task component to establish standard times, identify non-value-adding elements, and set the empirical baseline for all subsequent improvement activities.

trigger_conditions.description: CW cycle time exceeded 10% above baseline.
MW cycle time exceeded 10% above baseline.
Line balance below 75% — clear imbalance detected.
Worker idle fraction exceeds 35% — possible over-allocation.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Align takt time to observed cycle times per station.
Adjust pace to match measured human capacity.

nodes.label: Lean Production System
nodes.description: A holistic sociotechnical system that maximises customer value while minimising waste by aligning all production activities, people, and processes around flow, pull, and perfection — the integrative container for all other Lean methods.

trigger_conditions.description: OEE below 65% indicates systemic underperformance.
Throughput below 80% baseline — systemic review needed.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Establish balanced pull-based production rate.
Set takt time to customer demand rate.

nodes.label: Kaizen
nodes.description: A philosophy of continuous, small-step improvement driven by frontline workers using the PDCA cycle, applied daily to eliminate minor inefficiencies before they accumulate into systemic waste.

trigger_conditions.description: Throughput slips below 92% of baseline.
CW cycle time crept 8% above baseline.
Fatigue score above 55 — workload should be scrutinised.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Small incremental pace improvement after waste removal.

nodes.label: Fatigue Analysis (Muri / Overburden Identification)
nodes.description: Systematic identification and elimination of physical or cognitive overburden placed on workers, analysed via physiological indicators (HR, blink rate, fatigue classification) and task demand profiles, to redesign work within safe ergonomic envelopes per Industry 5.0.

trigger_conditions.description: Edge layer fatigue classifier triggered Warning state.
Mean HR exceeds 12% above individual worker baseline.
Physiological Load Index above 160 (substantially elevated).
Cumulative fatigue score above 65 threshold.
IBI increasing 30%+ above baseline — visual fatigue.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Move fatigued worker to lower-demand station; assign fresh worker to high-demand station (MW).
Reduce pace to bring physiological load to safe threshold.
Extend takt time to provide recovery intervals between cycles.

expected_kpi_impacts.note: Minor throughput cost accepted for safety-critical ergonomic intervention.

nodes.label: Root-Cause Analysis / Cause & Effect Analysis
nodes.description: Structured diagnosis using cause-and-effect mapping (4M/6M categories: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Environment) to trace observed defects or deviations to their true underlying causes, preventing symptom-only countermeasures.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect rate 50% above baseline — root cause investigation required.
Mean repair time exceeds 15 min — failure mode poorly understood.

expected_kpi_impacts.note: Impact realised after countermeasures are implemented.

nodes.label: 5 Whys
nodes.description: An iterative interrogation technique that asks 'why' up to five times to penetrate symptom layers and reach the systemic root cause. Forms the analytical backbone of A3 reports, Kaizen events, and DMAIC Analyse phase.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect rate 30% above baseline.
Repair time > 15 min.

nodes.label: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
nodes.description: Proactive risk assessment methodology that enumerates potential failure modes, their effects, and their causes; ranks them by Risk Priority Number (RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detectability); and drives preventive action plans before failures manifest.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect rate 20% above baseline — risk assessment required.
Any safety incident triggers FMEA.
MTBF degraded to 80% of baseline.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Reduce maintenance interval for highest-RPN failure modes.

nodes.label: Waste (Muda) Elimination
nodes.description: Systematic identification and removal of the eight categories of non-value-adding activity: Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Overprocessing, Defects, and underutilised Skills (TIMWOODS), to maximise the fraction of pure value-adding time.

trigger_conditions.description: 30%+ idle fraction indicates significant waiting waste.
Lead time 20% above baseline — non-value-add time accumulated.
OEE below 75% — significant waste present in system.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Waste removal frees capacity enabling pace increase.
Shorter cycle time after waste removal enables tighter takt.

nodes.label: Learning Curve Optimization
nodes.description: Application of Wright's Law and cumulative-production experience models to anticipate, track, and accelerate the worker performance improvement trajectory; used to calibrate ramp-up expectations and detect workers who plateau early or exhibit faster-than-expected habituation.

trigger_conditions.description: Early sessions — learning effects are dominant.
Cycle time 15% above steady-state baseline.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Gradually increase pace as learning curve flattens toward steady-state.
Assign most experienced worker to bottleneck station.

expected_kpi_impacts.note: Experienced workers show lower HR for same task intensity.

nodes.label: Continuous & Autonomous Improvement
nodes.description: An AI-augmented Kaizen philosophy in which agentic systems autonomously detect performance deviations, propose and simulate countermeasures via the digital twin, and deploy approved changes without waiting for scheduled improvement cycles — the operational mode of MAGI Layer 4.

trigger_conditions.description: Any throughput deviation triggers autonomous review.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Continuously calibrate based on real-time KPI feedback.
Dynamically reassign based on fatigue and skill profiles.

nodes.label: SQDCM Daily Management Dashboard
nodes.description: A five-dimensional daily management framework tracking Safety, Quality, Delivery, Cost, and Morale KPIs at shift level, providing immediate visibility against targets and routing deviations to the correct corrective methodology for each dimension.

trigger_conditions.description: OEE below 75% threshold.
Throughput 10% below baseline.

nodes.label: 5S Workplace Organisation
nodes.description: A systematic workplace organisation method using five sequential steps — Sort (Seiri), Set in Order (Seiton), Shine (Seiso), Standardize (Seiketsu), Sustain (Shitsuke) — to eliminate clutter, create visual standards, and establish the physical foundation for Lean improvement.

trigger_conditions.description: Any safety incident may indicate disorganised workstation.
Excess idle time may be motion/search waste.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Organised workstation reduces search and retrieval time, enabling pace increase.

nodes.label: A3 Problem Solving
nodes.description: A PDCA-based structured problem-solving approach condensed onto one A3-size sheet, comprising: background, current-state analysis, target state, root-cause analysis (gap analysis), countermeasures, implementation plan, and follow-up verification.

trigger_conditions.description: Structured problem-solving required when defects are persistently elevated.
Throughput below 88% baseline requiring structured analysis.

nodes.label: DMAIC Cycle
nodes.description: The core improvement methodology of Six Sigma: five structured phases (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) that reduce process variation to near-zero defect levels using statistical tools, measurement system analysis, and data-driven root cause investigation.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect rate doubled — statistical intervention required.
Cycle time variance beyond normal range.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Statistical optimisation of pace to reduce cycle time variance.

nodes.label: Kanban
nodes.description: A visual pull-based scheduling system using Kanban signals (cards, bins, digital triggers) to authorise production only when downstream demand exists, preventing overproduction and controlling work-in-progress inventory between the CW and MW stations.

trigger_conditions.description: Buffer queue exceeds 3 products — push behaviour detected.
Lead time 20% above baseline due to inventory accumulation.
MW near-saturation — CW feeding faster than MW can consume.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Constrain buffer to 1–3 units to enforce pull system.
Match CW production rate to MW consumption rate.

nodes.label: Poka-Yoke / Cognitive Poka-Yoke
nodes.description: Physical or algorithmic mechanisms that prevent errors before they become defects. Classical Poka-Yoke uses contact sensors, jigs, and interlocks; Cognitive Poka-Yoke extends this with AI-assisted real-time detection that flags operator assembly errors before they propagate downstream.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect rate 40% above baseline — error-proofing required.
First pass yield below 92%.

nodes.label: PDCA Cycle
nodes.description: The foundational iterative improvement cycle — Plan (define objective and hypothesis), Do (implement at small scale), Check (measure results against plan), Act (standardise success or restart) — that underlies all structured Lean and quality improvement methodologies including Kaizen, A3, and DMAIC.

trigger_conditions.description: Any significant deviation warrants a PDCA cycle.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Adjust in small increments per PDCA cycle; verify KPI response before next step.

nodes.label: Kaikaku (Radical Improvement)
nodes.description: A fundamental redesign or radical step-change of a production system, process, or layout — as opposed to the incremental approach of Kaizen — triggered when the current system has hit a structural ceiling that incremental improvement cannot break through.

trigger_conditions.description: Throughput below 80% despite repeated Kaizen attempts.
OEE below 60% — systemic structural change needed.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Structural redesign enables step-change capacity increase.
Kaikaku may involve robot upgrade or process redesign.
Radical redesign reconfigures role allocation.

nodes.label: Jidoka (Autonomation / Built-in Quality)
nodes.description: The principle of designing machines and processes to autonomously detect abnormalities and stop immediately, separating human work from machine work and preventing defect propagation. Jidoka is the quality pillar of TPS and enables Andon-based escalation. In MAGI, the cognitive agent embodies a digital Jidoka through real-time DT monitoring.

trigger_conditions.description: Defect elevation indicates undetected error source.
Low robot utilisation may indicate unconfigured quality checks.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Enable robot quality checks; may slightly reduce cycle speed but prevents defects.

nodes.label: Six Sigma
nodes.description: A data-driven quality management framework targeting process variation reduction to within 6 standard deviations of the specification limit (3.4 DPMO), using statistical tools and the DMAIC methodology. When combined with Lean it becomes Lean Six Sigma — addressing both waste (Lean) and variation (Six Sigma).

trigger_conditions.description: 50% defect rate elevation requires statistical process control.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Statistical optimisation of pace to minimise cycle time variance.

nodes.label: Heijunka (Production Levelling)
nodes.description: A production scheduling technique that levels both volume and product mix over time to eliminate Mura (unevenness) and reduce Muri (overburden). In the Silverline context, Heijunka is the primary tool for balancing CW and MW workloads, reducing queue build-up, and lowering Physiological Load Index by smoothing peak demand periods.

trigger_conditions.description: Line balance below 75% — Heijunka required.
Buffer queue > 3 units indicates CW–MW imbalance.
MW near saturation — bottleneck detected at MW.
CW under-utilised while MW is saturated.
Elevated PLI at MW due to uneven workload distribution.
Elevated fatigue state at MW operator.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Level CW output rate to match MW processing capacity.
Set unified takt time to smooth both stations.
Assign worker with lower MW PLI profile to the bottleneck station.
Set buffer capacity to allow Heijunka pacing without starving MW.

nodes.label: Value Stream Mapping (VSM)
nodes.description: An end-to-end visual mapping technique that captures all material and information flows for a product family, distinguishing value-adding from non-value-adding steps, and producing a future-state map that becomes the blueprint for targeted improvement actions.

trigger_conditions.description: Lead time 25% above baseline — flow analysis required.
Significant throughput loss — map system to identify constraint.

expected_kpi_impacts.note: Direct impact realised through VSM-guided improvement actions.

nodes.label: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
nodes.description: A comprehensive maintenance strategy that targets zero unplanned downtime through autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, and early equipment management. Directly improves OEE by addressing its Availability component, and eliminates losses from breakdowns, setup, and speed reduction.

trigger_conditions.description: Robot utilisation below 80% — equipment effectiveness issue.
MTBF degraded to 75% of baseline — proactive maintenance needed.
OEE below 70% — equipment-related losses dominant.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Increase maintenance frequency to restore MTBF.
Post-maintenance: recalibrate robot to optimal speed.

nodes.label: Hoshin Kanri / Catchball Process
nodes.description: A strategic management methodology that aligns organisational goals with operational improvement activities through the Catchball process — a two-way dialogue between management and frontline teams that translates annual breakthrough objectives into daily improvement priorities.

trigger_conditions.description: Strategic performance gap requiring policy-level alignment.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Strategic takt time aligned to breakthrough objective targets.
Shift structure aligned to strategic delivery targets.

nodes.label: Andon
nodes.description: A visual and audible signalling system that enables operators to immediately escalate detected problems — quality defects, equipment anomalies, line imbalance, or safety concerns — to supervisors and support functions in real time. In the MAGI framework, the Edge Layer fatigue alert is a digital Andon signal routed to the Cognitive Layer.

trigger_conditions.description: Quality anomaly detected.
Fatigue alert — MAGI digital Andon trigger.
Robot utilisation critically low — possible breakdown.

nodes.label: Standard Work (Standardized Work)
nodes.description: The precise, documented description of the safest, highest-quality, most efficient way to perform each task — defining takt time, work sequence, and standard in-process inventory. Standard Work is the baseline against which all Kaizen improvement is measured; without it there can be no sustained improvement.

trigger_conditions.description: Cycle time deviation suggests non-standard operation.

simulation_adjustments.rationale: Standard work defines the authoritative takt time.
Pace aligned to standard work sequence.

nodes.label: Production Bottleneck
nodes.description: One station limits system throughput; MW utilisation > 90%.

nodes.label: Operator Fatigue
nodes.description: Physiological overload causing risk to worker wellbeing and safety.

nodes.label: Excessive Cycle Time
nodes.description: Task duration exceeds standard time by > 10%.

nodes.label: Low Throughput
nodes.description: Units produced per hour below target by > 10%.

nodes.label: Quality Defect
nodes.description: Product fails in-process or end-of-line quality check.

nodes.label: Unplanned Downtime
nodes.description: Unexpected equipment or robot stop during production.

nodes.label: Line Imbalance
nodes.description: Significant mismatch in workload between CW and MW.

nodes.label: Excessive Waiting Waste
nodes.description: Queue length > 3 units; products waiting for MW.

nodes.label: Overproduction Waste
nodes.description: CW producing ahead of MW consumption rate (push behaviour).

nodes.label: Excess Inventory
nodes.description: Buffer exceeds optimal capacity; WIP accumulation.

nodes.label: Unnecessary Motion Waste
nodes.description: Non-value-adding operator movements (search, reach, reposition).

nodes.label: High Physiological Load
nodes.description: PLI > 150; sustained cardiovascular and cognitive overload.

nodes.label: Learning Inefficiency
nodes.description: Performance below steady-state learning curve expectation.

nodes.label: High Process Variability
nodes.description: Coefficient of variation of cycle time exceeds 25%.

nodes.label: Resource Underutilisation
nodes.description: Station utilisation below 60%; wasted capacity.

nodes.label: Safety Risk
nodes.description: Near-miss, ergonomic overload, or safety threshold breach.

nodes.label: Knowledge Loss / Skill Gap
nodes.description: Worker turnover or training gap causing performance degradation.

nodes.label: Transportation
nodes.description: Unnecessary movement of parts, products, or materials.

nodes.label: Inventory
nodes.description: Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods beyond immediate need.

nodes.label: Motion
nodes.description: Unnecessary operator movements not contributing to product value.

nodes.label: Waiting
nodes.description: Idle time due to imbalance, machine stops, or material shortages.

nodes.label: Overproduction
nodes.description: Producing more, sooner, or faster than downstream demand.

nodes.label: Overprocessing
nodes.description: Adding more value to a product than the customer requires.

nodes.label: Defects
nodes.description: Inspection, rework, and scrap from non-conforming products.

nodes.label: Underutilised Skills
nodes.description: Failing to use operator knowledge, skills, and creativity (8th waste).

nodes.label: Mura (Unevenness)
nodes.description: Irregular, uneven flow — peaks and valleys in demand or production.

nodes.label: Muri (Overburden)
nodes.description: Physical or cognitive overload placed on operators or equipment.

nodes.label: Non-Value-Add Processing
nodes.description: Processing steps that consume resources but add no customer value.

nodes.label: Takt Time
nodes.description: Available production time divided by customer demand rate.

nodes.label: One-Piece Flow
nodes.description: Processing one unit at a time through each operation without batching.

nodes.label: Pull System
nodes.description: Downstream demand triggers upstream production; prevents overproduction.

nodes.label: Standard Work
nodes.description: Defined sequence, timing, and inventory for each operation.

nodes.label: Visual Management
nodes.description: Making the state of processes immediately visible without verbal communication.

nodes.label: Just-in-Time (JIT)
nodes.description: Right part, right quantity, right time — no more, no less.

nodes.label: Autonomation
nodes.description: Machines that detect abnormalities and stop automatically.

nodes.label: Level Loading
nodes.description: Distributing work evenly across time and resources.

nodes.label: Cross-Training
nodes.description: Operators trained on multiple stations for flexible deployment.

nodes.label: Pacemaker Process
nodes.description: The single point in the value stream that sets the production rhythm.

nodes.label: Supermarket System
nodes.description: Controlled buffer between processes to enable pull-based replenishment.

nodes.label: Error-Proofing
nodes.description: Design mechanisms to prevent errors from becoming defects.

nodes.label: Robot Speed Factor
nodes.description: Float multiplier on robot action durations (1.0 = baseline; 1.2 = 20% faster).

nodes.label: Worker Assignment
nodes.description: Dict mapping worker IDs to stations: {cw_worker: '001', mw_worker: '002'}.

nodes.label: Production Pace Factor
nodes.description: Float multiplier on production rate (1.0 = baseline; 1.1 = 10% faster).

nodes.label: Takt Time
nodes.description: Target seconds per product unit (available time / demand rate).

nodes.label: CW→MW Buffer Capacity
nodes.description: Maximum products in the inter-station queue before CW must pause.

nodes.label: Shift Duration
nodes.description: Simulated shift length in hours.

nodes.label: Maintenance Interval
nodes.description: Number of products processed before preventive maintenance is triggered.

nodes.label: Just-in-Time
nodes.description: Right part, right amount, right time — the flow pillar of TPS.

nodes.label: Jidoka (Built-in Quality)
nodes.description: Stop on defect, separate human work from machine work.

nodes.label: Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)
nodes.description: Never-ending incremental improvement at every level.

nodes.label: Respect for People
nodes.description: Develop, engage, and respect employees and partners.

nodes.label: Heijunka (Level Production)
nodes.description: Smooth, predictable production rhythm eliminating Mura.

nodes.label: Human Centricity
nodes.description: Worker wellbeing, ergonomics, and human–robot collaboration.

nodes.label: Sustainability
nodes.description: Environmental responsibility, circular economy, energy efficiency.

nodes.label: Resilience
nodes.description: Adaptability, self-healing, and robustness to disruptions.

nodes.label: Technology Synergy
nodes.description: AI + human collaboration and cognitive augmentation.

nodes.label: Define
nodes.description: Define problem scope, project charter, SIPOC, and VOC.

nodes.label: Measure
nodes.description: Measure baseline performance; conduct MSA; establish capability.

nodes.label: Analyze
nodes.description: Analyze data to identify root causes; use 5 Whys, DOE, regression.

nodes.label: Improve
nodes.description: Generate and implement optimised solution; pilot and validate.

nodes.label: Control
nodes.description: Control plan, SPC charts, handover to process owner.

nodes.label: Plan
nodes.description: Define objective, hypothesis, and improvement approach.

nodes.label: Do
nodes.description: Implement at small scale under controlled conditions.

nodes.label: Check
nodes.description: Measure results against the planned objective.

nodes.label: Act
nodes.description: Standardise success or revise hypothesis and restart cycle.

nodes.label: Background
nodes.description: Context, urgency, and business reason for the problem.

nodes.label: Current State
nodes.description: Factual description of the current situation with data.

nodes.label: Target State
nodes.description: Desired future state and measurable targets.

nodes.label: Gap Analysis
nodes.description: Root-cause analysis of gap between current and target state.

nodes.label: Countermeasures
nodes.description: Proposed actions linked directly to root causes.

nodes.label: Implementation
nodes.description: Action plan with owner, timing, and resources.

nodes.label: Follow-Up
nodes.description: Verification of results and lessons learned.

nodes.label: Current State Map
nodes.description: Map actual material and information flows today.

nodes.label: Future State Map
nodes.description: Design ideal lean flow with wastes eliminated.

nodes.label: VSM Gap Analysis
nodes.description: Identify kaizen bursts — improvement priorities.

nodes.label: Implementation Plan
nodes.description: Phased action plan to move from current to future state.

links.description: Time study data is input for Heijunka scheduling.
Measured times form the standard work definition.
Kaizen requires the PDCA structure to avoid unsustained changes.
Kaizen improves; Standard Work sustains the improvement.
When incremental Kaizen cannot break through a structural barrier, escalate to Kaikaku.
Complex multi-root problems exceed Kaizen scope — use A3.
Identifying overburden drives load-levelling via Heijunka.
Structural ergonomic overload not solvable by pace adjustment alone.
When RCA reveals a statistical variation problem, escalate to DMAIC.
When 5 Whys is insufficient, escalate to full Ishikawa/fishbone RCA.
FMEA error mode analysis drives Poka-Yoke device specification.
FMEA identifies critical failure modes for TPM prioritisation.

links.note: HR as proxy for learning stage.

links.description: Autonomous improvement needs a defined standard as baseline.
MAGI automates Kaizen detection and deployment.
SQDCM tracks trends; Andon handles real-time escalations.
A3 gap analysis section requires root cause methodology.
DMAIC is the execution methodology of Six Sigma.
Kanban pull system requires levelled production upstream.
Poka-Yoke devices are specified from FMEA failure modes.
PDCA is the structured framework within which Kaizen executes.
Radical redesign requires current-state VSM as baseline.
Jidoka autonomous detection enables Andon escalation.
Jidoka stops on defect; Andon escalates the stop.
Lean eliminates waste; Six Sigma reduces variation — Lean Six Sigma.
Heijunka scheduling requires accurate task time data.
Heijunka levels production; Kanban controls pull — together they eliminate Mura and Muda.
Both target overburden from different angles.
VSM identifies wastes before elimination begins.
VSM current-state exposes imbalance for Heijunka to resolve.
VSM defines supermarket locations for Kanban design.
VSM kaizen bursts identify targeted improvement areas.
Strategic alignment via Hoshin precedes radical redesign.
Policy deployment requires performance monitoring infrastructure.

links.note: MAGI Andon = edge layer fatigue alert

links.description: Repeated Andon triggers warrant root cause investigation.
Standard work establishes the baseline Kaizen improves upon.
Standard work is built from time study measurements.

links.condition: IBI paradoxical increase under extreme load
High saccade variance correlates with attention variability
